sound_type: high-to-low spine-chilling scream -- full rutting bugle: three-phase vocalization: (1) high-pitched opening whistles 2000-4000 Hz; (2) descending haunting scream 400-800 Hz; (3) deep closing grunts 50-200 Hz; full spectrum 50-4000 Hz; involuntary human response; carries 1-2 miles in still Olympic Peninsula morning air with temperature inversion
ecosystem: River valley -- Olympic Peninsula old-growth river valley forest; Hoh, Quinault, Queets, Dosewallips valleys; dense Sitka spruce/western hemlock/bigleaf maple; fall morning temperature inversions; September-October mist and fog acoustic landscape
season: Fall (rut) -- September-October peak; photoperiod-triggered testosterone surge; most acoustically intense period; bulls bugle to advertise fitness, challenge rivals, establish harem dominance
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Olympic Peninsula population approximately 5,000 -- largest US Roosevelt Elk population. Managed as game species in WA and OR. Olympic National Park provides core habitat protection.
milestone: APEX PREDATOR ARC GATEWAY. Final sixth opens. Three consecutive E=9 entries follow (302-304). Gateway from E=7 five-sixths mark (degree 300) to E=8 final sixth opening. Prey species precedes predator species in ecological sequence.

coordinates.description: Olympic Peninsula core range: Hoh, Quinault, Queets, Dosewallips river valleys. Also Coast Range OR, Cascades foothills WA, Willapa Hills. Named for Theodore Roosevelt who established Olympic Peninsula preserve 1909.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Vocal -- larynx-produced multi-phase bugle. Three phases: high-pitched opening whistles, descending haunting scream, deep closing grunts. Testosterone-dependent behavior: only rutting bulls produce full bugle.
acoustic_data.mechanics: Progressive laryngeal muscle relaxation produces characteristic descending pitch slide. Chest resonance amplifies lower-frequency grunts. Bulls 700-1100 lbs produce larger, more resonant bugles advertising superior fitness.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 50-4,000 Hz full audible spectrum. Phase 1 (whistles): 2,000-4,000 Hz. Phase 2 (descending scream): 400-800 Hz. Phase 3 (grunts): 50-200 Hz. Spectral breadth is the acoustic signature.
acoustic_data.duration: Full bugle: 5-15 seconds for complete three-phase sequence. Peak rut: bulls may bugle hundreds of times per day.
acoustic_data.carry_distance: 1-2 miles in favorable conditions: still morning air, temperature inversion, Olympic river valley corridor. Mid-range descending scream carries best through forest cover.
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8: three-phase full-spectrum vocalization; involuntary human response; 1-2 mile carry; fall rut peak intensity. Not E=9: seasonal communication signal, not alarm/threat maximum. Gateway to three consecutive E=9 predator entries.

nodes.title: Apex Predator Arc Gateway -- Final Sixth Opens
nodes.description: Roosevelt Elk at E=8 is the gateway to three consecutive E=9 entries (302-304: Cougar, Gray Wolf, Grizzly Bear). The final sixth opens with E=8 -- escalation from E=7 at the five-sixths mark. The prey species announces the arc; the predators follow.

nodes.title: Prey-Predator Ecological Sequence
nodes.description: The arc begins with a prey species (Cervidae, E=8) and transitions to apex predators (Felidae, Canidae, Ursidae, all E=9). Ecologically correct: the elk's presence in the river valley is what the predators are responding to. The prey announces itself acoustically; the predators are present.

nodes.title: Three-Phase Bugle -- Full Spectrum
nodes.description: The full bugle spans 50-4,000 Hz across three distinct phases. The descending scream (Phase 2) is the signature component: 400-800 Hz, involuntary human response, maximum forest penetration. The spectral breadth gives the bugle its multi-dimensional acoustic presence.

nodes.title: Olympic Peninsula River Valley Acoustic Landscape
nodes.description: Hoh, Quinault, Queets, Dosewallips valleys: wide flat corridors, old-growth flanks, temperature inversions. Multiple bulls bugling simultaneously creates 3D acoustic landscape. Salmon run co-timing in September-October: elk rut and salmon return in same river systems simultaneously.

cross_references.relationship: Next degree. E=9. Felidae. First of three consecutive E=9. Cougar is primary predator of Roosevelt Elk on Olympic Peninsula.
E=9. FIRST CANIDAE. Pack predator of elk herds. Gray wolves reintroduced in WA -- primary prey: elk.
E=9. Third consecutive max. Closes apex predator arc.
Previous degree. E=7. Five-sixths mark. E=7→E=8: final sixth escalation confirmed.
E=6 + E=8 = E=14. Noise rock confrontation pairs with spine-chilling river valley bugle. Final sixth opens on both tracks.
